Maladaptive Strategies.value: deliberately try to leave my body or blank out
I distract myself with something else
I try to erase the memory of the event

Threat.value: Having strong physical reactions when something reminded you of the stressful experience
I frequently look behind me to check if someone is following me
I am unable to relax even when I know I am in a safe place

Memory.value: My memories of the event feel more real and vivid than memories of ordinary things
My memories of the frightening event are very clear and detailed.
When I remember the event I feel overwhelming grief

Negative Appraisals.value: Nothing good can happen to me anymore
The people responsible have faced no consequences

vignette:
Marilyn, a 79-year-old North American single judge, presented with persistent fear, vivid recollection, and deliberate attempts to blank out the aftermath of a rape she experienced years ago. She describes the assault as happening when she was alone and believed she was safe; at the point of peak danger, she was physically overpowered and unable to stop the attack. That experience has left her especially uneasy in the courthouse and in the parking area when she is walking to her car and notices someone behind her.

When Marilyn remembers the assault, she does not describe it as something distant or faded. She says it feels more real than ordinary events, with the details coming back sharply and clearly, and she can be overtaken by grief while speaking about it. In session, she may stare ahead and go quiet, as though pulled back into the moment rather than recalling it from a distance.

Her central stuck point is, in her words, that nothing good can happen to her anymore, that she has no future, and that the people responsible have faced no consequences. She speaks about this with resignation and bitterness, as if the world has already confirmed it. Her belief that nothing good can happen to her anymore keeps her on constant alert for danger and unable to relax.

Marilyn frequently looks behind her when moving through the courthouse or across the parking lot, and she finds herself unable to settle even when she is at home or in her own chambers. The people responsible have faced no consequences, and that leaves her expecting harm whenever she is alone in public. Her memories of the assault feel more real and vivid than ordinary memories, and they are marked by overwhelming grief when they surface.

Marilyn deliberately tries to leave her body, distract herself with paperwork, and erase the memory from mind. Her fear that nothing good can happen to her anymore keeps those efforts in place when the memories and bodily reactions become intense. In court, this has shown up in small but visible pauses: Marilyn checks behind her before crossing the parking lot, then slows at the door before going back to work.

vignette_attempts.vignette:
Marilyn, a 79-year-old North American single judge, presented with persistent fear, intrusive recollection, and a sense of emotional shutdown since being raped years earlier. She describes the assault as occurring in a setting where she believed she should have been safe and in control; at the point of greatest danger, she was physically overpowered and unable to stop what was happening. She says she did not know whether anyone would come to help, and that helplessness remains central in how she experiences the event. In court and on her way through the courthouse parking area, she is especially unsettled by moments that resemble being approached from behind, and by times when she has to move through quiet, enclosed spaces alone.

When Marilyn recalls the assault, she does not tell it like an ordinary memory. She says it comes back “too real,” as if she is back in the room rather than thinking about it from the present. She can give a detailed account of the sequence and the sensations, but it arrives with a force that makes her stop speaking and stare ahead. At those moments, she also notices overwhelming grief, and it feels less like remembering and more like being pulled into the same terror and loss.

Her central stuck point is that the attack proved to her that “nothing good can happen to me anymore,” that she has “no future,” and that the people who harmed her have faced no consequences. She speaks with a flat, bitter certainty, as though the world has already shown her what it is.

Marilyn is frequently on guard. She often looks behind her when she leaves chambers or walks to her car, and she finds it hard to settle even in her own home. When her body reacts sharply to an unexpected reminder, she tries to blank out, distract herself with paperwork, or push the memory away altogether.

These reactions appear to keep one another going. Her belief that nothing good lies ahead and that no one was held accountable keeps her alert for danger and unable to relax. That persistent vigilance makes the memory return with greater force and vividness, and the memory then deepens her grief and sense of futility. In turn, when the memory and fear rise, Marilyn deliberately tries to leave her body, distract herself, or erase the event from mind. She reports that these efforts give only brief relief, and the memory returns with the same sharp detail. At work, this has affected her composure in chambers; she sometimes pauses before walking alone to her car, checking behind her more than once before moving on.

violations.explanation: The vignette explicitly links situations that resemble being approached from behind or moving through enclosed spaces to her checking/avoidance behaviors: "In court and on her way through the courthouse parking area, she is especially unsettled by moments that resemble being approached from behind, and by times when she has to move through quiet, enclosed spaces alone." This is a single sentence naming a trigger-like situation and subsequent strategy context, but the sentence does not contain explicit causal language directly connecting Triggers to Maladaptive Strategies. Because explicit causal language between the two is not present, this forbidden edge is absent.
violations.quote: In court and on her way through the courthouse parking area, she is especially unsettled by moments that resemble being approached from behind, and by times when she has to move through quiet, enclosed spaces alone.

violations.explanation: The vignette mentions trigger-like situations and fear, but not in a single sentence with explicit causal language from Triggers to Threat. The sentence "In court and on her way through the courthouse parking area, she is especially unsettled by moments that resemble being approached from behind, and by times when she has to move through quiet, enclosed spaces alone." contains both the situations and distress, yet lacks a grammatical causal connector like 'causes' or 'leads to' between them. Therefore this forbidden edge is absent.
violations.quote: In court and on her way through the courthouse parking area, she is especially unsettled by moments that resemble being approached from behind, and by times when she has to move through quiet, enclosed spaces alone.

violations.explanation: The vignette says reminders can make the memory return, but the relevant sentence is not an explicit single-sentence causal statement from Triggers to Memory. The phrase "When her body reacts sharply to an unexpected reminder, she tries to blank out, distract herself with paperwork, or push the memory away altogether" places reminder and memory in the same sentence, but the causal language is directed toward her coping response, not from triggers to memory. Under the required standard, this forbidden edge is absent.
violations.quote: When her body reacts sharply to an unexpected reminder, she tries to blank out, distract herself with paperwork, or push the memory away altogether.

violations.explanation: There is no single sentence with explicit causal language showing triggers producing negative appraisals. The sentence about reminders and coping says: "When her body reacts sharply to an unexpected reminder, she tries to blank out, distract herself with paperwork, or push the memory away altogether." This does not state that the reminder causes the belief "nothing good can happen to me anymore." Thus the forbidden edge is absent.
violations.quote: When her body reacts sharply to an unexpected reminder, she tries to blank out, distract herself with paperwork, or push the memory away altogether.

violations.explanation: Although her belief that "nothing good lies ahead" is said to keep her alert and unable to relax, no single sentence explicitly states that the negative appraisal causes the maladaptive strategies. The sentence "Her belief that nothing good lies ahead and that no one was held accountable keeps her alert for danger and unable to relax" links appraisal to vigilance, not directly to strategies like blanking out or distracting herself. Therefore this forbidden edge is not present by the specified standard.
violations.quote: Her belief that nothing good lies ahead and that no one was held accountable keeps her alert for danger and unable to relax.

violations.explanation: The vignette does not contain a single sentence explicitly stating that her negative appraisals cause triggers. The appraisals are described as her stuck point, while the trigger-like situations are described separately; no grammatical causal connector links the appraisal to those situations. So this forbidden edge is absent.
violations.quote: Her belief that nothing good lies ahead and that no one was held accountable keeps her alert for danger and unable to relax.

violations.explanation: The text does say that when the memory and fear rise, Marilyn tries to leave her body, distract herself, or erase the event from mind. However, the sentence combines memory and strategies without explicit causal language directly from memory to strategies in the required form. The causal wording is broader and reciprocal, not a single clear A→B statement. Therefore this forbidden edge is absent.
violations.quote: In turn, when the memory and fear rise, Marilyn deliberately tries to leave her body, distract herself, or erase the event from mind.

violations.explanation: The vignette states that the memory deepens her grief and sense of futility, but it does not explicitly say in one sentence that the memory causes threat. No sentence contains both terms with a causal connector from Memory to Threat. Thus this forbidden edge is absent.
violations.quote: The memory then deepens her grief and sense of futility.

violations.explanation: While her memory and grief are linked to her sense of futility, the vignette does not contain a single sentence that explicitly says memory causes her negative appraisals. The stuck point is described independently. Therefore this forbidden edge is absent.
violations.quote: The memory then deepens her grief and sense of futility.

violations.explanation: The vignette includes reminders and memories, but no single sentence with explicit causal language from memory to triggers. The described sentence about the memory returning does not show memory causing triggers. So this forbidden edge is absent.
violations.quote: The memory returns with the same sharp detail.

violations.explanation: The vignette describes fear and vigilance, but does not explicitly state in one sentence that threat causes triggers. There is no sentence with both components and a causal connector between them. Hence the forbidden edge is absent.
violations.quote: Her belief that nothing good lies ahead and that no one was held accountable keeps her alert for danger and unable to relax.

violations.explanation: The vignette does not explicitly say that her coping behaviors cause her negative beliefs. The sentence about efforts giving brief relief and the memory returning does not connect strategies to appraisals. Therefore this forbidden edge is absent.
violations.quote: She reports that these efforts give only brief relief, and the memory returns with the same sharp detail.

satisfied_edges.explanation: The sentence explicitly states that her negative appraisal increases vigilance and inability to relax, which is threat-related arousal, using causal language "keeps" between the appraisal and the threat state.
satisfied_edges.quote: Her belief that nothing good lies ahead and that no one was held accountable keeps her alert for danger and unable to relax.

satisfied_edges.explanation: The sentence explicitly says her belief makes the memory return with greater force and vividness, so the negative appraisal is causally linked to memory in the same sentence with clear causal language.
satisfied_edges.quote: Her belief that nothing good lies ahead and that no one was held accountable keeps her alert for danger and unable to relax. That persistent vigilance makes the memory return with greater force and vividness, and the memory then deepens her grief and sense of futility.

satisfied_edges.explanation: The vignette states that when her memory and fear rise, she deliberately uses avoidance/dissociation strategies. Fear is a threat-related state, and the sentence uses explicit causal language "when" to indicate that threat rising leads to maladaptive strategies.
satisfied_edges.quote: In turn, when the memory and fear rise, Marilyn deliberately tries to leave her body, distract herself, or erase the event from mind.

satisfied_edges.explanation: The text says her persistent vigilance makes the memory return with greater force and vividness; vigilance is a threat state, and the sentence explicitly uses causal language "makes" between the threat-related state and memory.
satisfied_edges.quote: That persistent vigilance makes the memory return with greater force and vividness, and the memory then deepens her grief and sense of futility.

satisfied_edges.explanation: The vignette says the memory and fear deepen her grief and sense of futility, but this does not clearly show threat causing negative appraisals. The strongest matching sentence is the one about her belief keeping her alert for danger, which is the reverse direction. No single sentence explicitly states that threat causes the negative belief, so this required edge is missing.
satisfied_edges.quote: Her belief that nothing good lies ahead and that no one was held accountable keeps her alert for danger and unable to relax.

satisfied_edges.explanation: The vignette explicitly says her efforts to leave her body, distract herself, or erase the event from mind give only brief relief, and then the memory returns with the same sharp detail. This is explicit causal language showing maladaptive strategies are followed by the memory’s return, with the causal relation in the same sentence sequence.
satisfied_edges.quote: In turn, when the memory and fear rise, Marilyn deliberately tries to leave her body, distract herself, or erase the event from mind. She reports that these efforts give only brief relief, and the memory returns with the same sharp detail.

vignette_attempts.vignette:
Marilyn, a 79-year-old North American single judge, presented with persistent fear, vivid recollection, and deliberate attempts to blank out the aftermath of a rape she experienced years ago. She describes the assault as happening when she was alone and believed she was safe; at the point of peak danger, she was physically overpowered and unable to stop the attack. That experience has left her especially uneasy in the courthouse and in the parking area when she is walking to her car and notices someone behind her.

When Marilyn remembers the assault, she does not describe it as something distant or faded. She says it feels more real than ordinary events, with the details coming back sharply and clearly, and she can be overtaken by grief while speaking about it. In session, she may stare ahead and go quiet, as though pulled back into the moment rather than recalling it from a distance.

Her central stuck point is, in her words, that nothing good can happen to her anymore, that she has no future, and that the people responsible have faced no consequences. She speaks about this with resignation and bitterness, as if the world has already confirmed it.

Marilyn frequently looks behind her when moving through the courthouse or across the parking lot, and she finds herself unable to relax even when she is at home or in her own chambers. When her body reacts strongly to something that reminds her of the rape, she deliberately tries to leave her body, distract herself with paperwork, or erase the memory from mind.

Her belief that nothing good can happen to her anymore and that she has no future keeps her on constant alert for danger. The people responsible have faced no consequences, and that leaves her expecting danger and unable to settle. Her strong physical reactions to reminders of the rape keep the memory active, and the memory’s vividness and realism increase her grief when it returns. Her efforts to blank out, distract herself, and push the memory away are driven by that threat state, and those strategies leave the memory still sharp when it comes back. In court, this has shown up in small but visible pauses: Marilyn checks behind her before crossing the parking lot, then slows at the door before going back to work.

violations.explanation: The sentence 'When her body reacts strongly to something that reminds her of the rape, she deliberately tries to leave her body, distract herself with paperwork, or erase the memory from mind.' explicitly names a trigger-like reminder and maladaptive strategies, with causal language ('When ...') linking the reminder to the strategies in the same sentence.
violations.quote: When her body reacts strongly to something that reminds her of the rape, she deliberately tries to leave her body, distract herself with paperwork, or erase the memory from mind.

violations.explanation: The sentence 'Her strong physical reactions to reminders of the rape keep the memory active...' names reminders of the rape and an ongoing threat state, with explicit causal wording that connects the reminder-triggered reaction to the threat state in the same sentence.
violations.quote: Her efforts to blank out, distract herself, and push the memory away are driven by that threat state, and those strategies leave the memory still sharp when it comes back.

violations.explanation: The sentence 'Her strong physical reactions to reminders of the rape keep the memory active, and the memory’s vividness and realism increase her grief when it returns.' explicitly includes reminders of the rape and the memory, with causal language ('keep') linking the reminder-triggered reactions to the memory being active in the same sentence.
violations.quote: Her strong physical reactions to reminders of the rape keep the memory active, and the memory’s vividness and realism increase her grief when it returns.

violations.explanation: The sentence 'Her efforts to blank out, distract herself, and push the memory away are driven by that threat state...' links threat state to strategies, but not negative appraisals to strategies in a single sentence. This forbidden edge is not explicitly present and should be absent, not violated.
No single sentence explicitly states that negative appraisals cause triggers. The vignette has negative appraisals and reminders, but not with the required causal language between them.
No single sentence explicitly says memory causes negative appraisals. The appraisal statement is separate from the memory description.
No single sentence explicitly states that memory causes triggers. The vignette references reminders and memory separately without that causal direction.

satisfied_edges.explanation: The sentence 'Her belief that nothing good can happen to her anymore and that she has no future keeps her on constant alert for danger.' explicitly links negative appraisals to threat with causal language ('keeps') between them in the same sentence.
satisfied_edges.quote: Her belief that nothing good can happen to her anymore and that she has no future keeps her on constant alert for danger.

satisfied_edges.explanation: The sentence 'Her strong physical reactions to reminders of the rape keep the memory active, and the memory’s vividness and realism increase her grief when it returns.' does not show this edge. However, the sentence 'Her belief that nothing good can happen to her anymore and that she has no future keeps her on constant alert for danger.' contains no memory link either. The required causal link from negative appraisals to memory is not explicitly present.
The sentence 'Her efforts to blank out, distract herself, and push the memory away are driven by that threat state...' explicitly states that the threat state drives the maladaptive strategies.
satisfied_edges.quote: Her efforts to blank out, distract herself, and push the memory away are driven by that threat state, and those strategies leave the memory still sharp when it comes back.

satisfied_edges.explanation: The sentence 'Her strong physical reactions to reminders of the rape keep the memory active...' does not state threat to memory. The required edge is not explicitly present.

The sentence 'Her belief that nothing good can happen to her anymore and that she has no future keeps her on constant alert for danger.' shows negative appraisals causing threat, not threat causing negative appraisals. The required reverse causal link is missing.

The sentence 'Her efforts to blank out, distract herself, and push the memory away are driven by that threat state, and those strategies leave the memory still sharp when it comes back.' explicitly states that the strategies leave the memory still sharp, which is a causal effect on memory in the same sentence.
satisfied_edges.quote: Her efforts to blank out, distract herself, and push the memory away are driven by that threat state, and those strategies leave the memory still sharp when it comes back.

- Triggers → Maladaptive Strategies: The sentence 'When her body reacts strongly to something that reminds her of the rape, she deliberately tries to leave her body, distract herself with paperwork, or erase the memory from mind.' explicitly names a trigger-like reminder and maladaptive strategies, with causal language ('When ...') linking the reminder to the strategies in the same sentence.

- Negative Appraisals → Maladaptive Strategies: The sentence 'Her efforts to blank out, distract herself, and push the memory away are driven by that threat state...' links threat state to strategies, but not negative appraisals to strategies in a single sentence. vignette_attempts.vignette:
Marilyn, a 79-year-old North American single judge, presented with persistent fear, vivid recollection, and deliberate attempts to blank out the aftermath of a rape she experienced years ago. She describes the assault as happening when she was alone and believed she was safe; at the point of peak danger, she was physically overpowered and unable to stop the attack. That experience has left her especially uneasy in the courthouse and in the parking area when she is walking to her car and notices someone behind her.

When Marilyn remembers the assault, she does not describe it as something distant or faded. She says it feels more real than ordinary events, with the details coming back sharply and clearly, and she can be overtaken by grief while speaking about it. In session, she may stare ahead and go quiet, as though pulled back into the moment rather than recalling it from a distance.

Her central stuck point is, in her words, that nothing good can happen to her anymore, that she has no future, and that the people responsible have faced no consequences. She speaks about this with resignation and bitterness, as if the world has already confirmed it. Her belief that nothing good can happen to her anymore keeps her on constant alert for danger and unable to relax.

Marilyn frequently looks behind her when moving through the courthouse or across the parking lot, and she finds herself unable to settle even when she is at home or in her own chambers. The people responsible have faced no consequences, and that leaves her expecting harm whenever she is alone in public. Her memories of the assault feel more real and vivid than ordinary memories, and they are marked by overwhelming grief when they surface.

Marilyn deliberately tries to leave her body, distract herself with paperwork, and erase the memory from mind. Her fear that nothing good can happen to her anymore keeps those efforts in place when the memories and bodily reactions become intense. In court, this has shown up in small but visible pauses: Marilyn checks behind her before crossing the parking lot, then slows at the door before going back to work.

violations.explanation: The vignette states negative beliefs and fear separately, but not in a single sentence with explicit causal language linking the negative appraisals to threat. A required sentence would need to say, for example, that her belief that nothing good can happen to her causes or keeps her feeling threatened.

The vignette includes negative appraisals and vivid memories, but no single sentence explicitly says the negative appraisals cause or shape the memory experience. A required edge would need direct causal wording connecting her beliefs to her memories.

Threat-related behavior is present, but there is no single sentence explicitly stating that threat leads to her maladaptive strategies. A required sentence would need causal language such as threat causing her to check behind her or distract herself.

The vignette describes fear and memories, but does not contain a single sentence with explicit causal language showing that threat produces or intensifies memory. A required sentence would need to state that feeling threatened causes the memories to surface or become vivid.

The vignette presents threat and negative beliefs, but not in one sentence where explicit causal language shows threat leading to the negative appraisals. A required sentence would need wording like threat causing her belief that nothing good can happen to her.

The vignette mentions deliberate efforts to blank out the aftermath of the rape and vivid recollection, but not a single sentence with explicit causal language showing maladaptive strategies affecting memory. A required sentence would need to say those strategies keep the memory active, block it, or otherwise alter it.
